method3.generated_article:
==Geography==
Kavachi is a submarine volcano located in the southwest Pacific Ocean, situated approximately 24 km south of Vangunu Island in the western Solomon Islands.[2][3] The volcano lies within the tectonically active region known as the Pacific Ring of Fire.[1][3]

Kavachi formed at a convergent plate boundary, where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Solomon Plate.[2] The seamount is situated only about 30 km from the boundary of the Indian and Australian tectonic plates within the Solomon Island chain of volcanoes.[4] This active tectonic setting makes it highly prone to frequent volcanic activity.[3]

The volcano is a roughly conical feature rising more than 1,200 m from the surrounding seafloor, with a base approximately eight kilometers in diameter.[2][4] Its summit lies only about 20 m below sea level, shallow enough that eruptions produce discoloured plumes of water visible from aircraft and satellites.[2] The flanks of the edifice descend at relatively uniform 18° slopes to depths greater than 1,000 m.[5]

The summit of Kavachi features an oblong, pockmarked crater measuring approximately 120 m × 75 m, with a length-wise strike of 115° and a rim rising to an average depth of 24 m below sea level.[5] The deepest soundings on the peak reach approximately 70 m water depth, revealing asymmetrical terrain within the crater.[5] A secondary summit, sometimes referred to as the "southwest extension," has been confirmed 1.3 km southwest of the main summit, rising to a depth of 260 m and separated from the main peak by a narrow col.[5] This second peak displays evidence of diffuse-flow hydrothermal venting.[5]

Due to its shallow depth and frequent phreatomagmatic activity, Kavachi has produced ephemeral islands at least twice in the past century, with eruptions occasionally breaching the sea surface to create short-lived volcanic landmasses.[3][4] The remote geographic location and inherent danger posed by frequent eruptions have historically restricted scientific access to the volcano's summit crater.[5]

==Eruptions==
Kavachi is one of the most active submarine volcanoes in the Pacific Ocean, with near-surface eruptions occurring every few years that often build temporary islands.[1] Since its first recorded eruption in 1939, new islands have formed on at least nine occasions, though none have been large enough or sufficiently armored with solid lava flows to resist wave erosion, and each has been submerged within months of its formation.[1]

According to the Global Volcanism Program, Kavachi has had 33 historic eruptions, spanning a total of 44 out of 87 years, meaning the volcano is active roughly one in every two years on average.[1] This figure is likely an underestimate, as the historical record may not capture all eruptive events, particularly older ones.[1] The most recent eruption began in 2021 and was still ongoing as of May 2026.[1]

== Historical eruptions ==

In late 1951, small tsunamis occurred frequently in association with submarine activity at Kavachi, with one possible date of 22 December 1951 noted in the literature.[1]

On 14 May 2000, scientists aboard the CSIRO research vessel Franklin arrived at Kavachi and documented a new phase of island-building eruptive activity, following a nine-year period of dormancy.[2] Eruptions on that date ejected ash and incandescent blocks of lava up to 70 m above sea level, while sulfurous steam plumes rose to approximately 500 m.[2] At that time, the peak of the volcano was forming a sandy ashen beach about two meters below sea level.[2]

Explosive eruptions occurred daily between 27 November and 13 December 2001.[2] On 13 January 2002, volcanic debris and gas bubbles were observed rising from the submarine volcano, accompanied by frequent loud noises, with the activity being more vigorous than that seen in December 2001.[2]

During an eruptive cycle reported in November 2003, a 15 m-high island formed at Kavachi, though by 16 November the summit had been reduced to approximately 32 m below sea level, and for the first time in four years no evidence of volcanic activity was observed.[2] On 15 March 2004, renewed volcanic activity was observed following a period of quiescence.[2]

== 2015 lull and scientific expedition ==

An expedition to Kavachi in January 2015 coincided with a rare lull in volcanic activity, permitting scientists to access the inside of the active crater and its flanks for the first time.[3][5] The isolated location of the volcano and its typically explosive behavior had previously restricted scientific access to surface imagery and peripheral water-column measurements.[3] During the lull, researchers collected medium-resolution bathymetry, benthic imagery, biological observations, petrological and geochemical samples from the crater rim, and measurements of water temperature, gas flux, and the hydrothermal plume structure.[3][5]

The 2015 expedition confirmed the existence of a previously only speculated secondary peak to the southwest of the main summit, which exhibited diffuse-flow venting.[3][4] Researchers found that Kavachi has an elongated crater measuring 120 by 75 m, surrounded by a 24 m high rim, with a summit sitting 24 m below sea level, while the secondary peak lies 260 m below sea level.[4] Volcaniclastic rocks collected from the summit indicated that volatile degassing partly drove the basaltic and mafic eruptions, and elemental analysis suggested that the magma source is likely a depleted forearc mantle rather than one influenced by subducted slab fluids.[4] Carbon dioxide gas and warm water were observed venting from inside the crater.[4]

==Marine life==
Despite the extreme conditions within its crater, Kavachi is notable for hosting a surprisingly diverse marine ecosystem, earning it the nickname "Sharkcano".[2] A 2015 scientific expedition documented a thriving community of organisms within the active crater, including gelatinous zooplankton, reef fish, microbial communities and large marine predators.[5][4] The finding captured significant public attention, as the idea of predators inhabiting an active volcanic vent challenged conventional assumptions about marine habitability.[5]

## Shark populations

Among the most remarkable inhabitants of the Kavachi crater are two species of shark: the scalloped hammerhead shark (*Sphyrna lewini*) and the silky shark (*Carcharhinus falciformis*).[4] These sharks were filmed swimming inside the active crater and displayed aggressive behaviour toward bait deployed by underwater cameras.[4] Their presence within such a hostile environment has raised significant scientific questions about the physiological and behavioural tolerance limits of large marine predators.[4]

## Environmental conditions in the crater

The crater waters at Kavachi are characterised by high temperatures, low oxygen levels, high acidity, and elevated concentrations of volcanic gases.[4] Measurements above the crater have recorded temperature increases of up to 13 °C above surrounding waters, reaching approximately 42 °C, with pH levels estimated around 6.1, indicating highly acidic conditions.[4] Some sources report even more extreme acidity, with crater pH values as low as 1–2, along with elevated temperatures and dissolved minerals.[2] The hydrothermal plume contains suspended particles that provide an ideal habitat for specialised microbial populations, including chemosynthetic bacteria of the genus *Sulfurimonas*, which metabolise sulfur and carbon dioxide.[4]

## Adaptations and survival mechanisms

How large predators such as sharks survive inside Kavachi remains an open scientific question.[1] Proposed explanations include behavioural adaptation, whereby sharks enter the crater during quiescent phases and depart before eruptions; enhanced sensory perception allowing detection of chemical or seismic precursors to eruptions; and physiological tolerance to heat and acidity.[1] It is not yet clear whether the sharks reside permanently within the crater or cycle through the site periodically, and long-term monitoring is required to resolve this question.[5]

## Scientific significance

Researchers have described Kavachi as a "natural laboratory" for the study of extreme marine ecosystems, as the presence of large vertebrates in its crater suggests that certain marine species may tolerate rapid environmental shifts more readily than previously assumed.[5] The volcano provides valuable opportunities to investigate submarine volcanism, adaptation of life to heat and acidity, and the release of greenhouse gases from underwater volcanoes.[1] Robotic missions have documented sudden drops in ocean pH, temperatures up to 10 °C above normal, and the release of volcanic gases into seawater, offering insights into how marine organisms may respond to warming and acidifying oceans under climate change.[1] The sharks of Kavachi, in particular, may provide clues about how species can adapt to increasingly hostile marine environments.[1]
